prompt: Cinematic documentary photograph of a lone figure descending a steep mountain slope in dense fog. The figure leans forward into the descent, using a long wooden walking stick to probe the ground immediately ahead — each placement testing the slope underfoot before committing their weight. The fog is thick and close, cutting visibility to a few metres in every direction; the figure can see only the immediate terrain beneath their feet and stick. Their body is angled decisively downhill toward a shallow depression visible just ahead. In the far background, a single dramatic break in the fog reveals a much deeper, wider valley far below — the figure is facing away from it, entirely unaware. The deeper valley is lit by a shaft of cold light through the fog gap, making it clearly visible to the viewer but not to the descending figure. Late afternoon mountain light. Cinematic wide-angle lens, shot from slightly above and behind the figure. Muted greens and greys, cold fog tones, single warm shaft of distant light. No diagrams, no graphs, no text, no labels, no mathematical notation, no equations, no floating annotations, no non-physical elements, no neural network visualisations, no data flow arrows, no UI elements, no screen displays, no infographic elements. Everything in the scene must be a physical object that could exist in the real world. Documentary photography style — this must look like a frame from a nature documentary.
metaphor: Fog-bound walker probes only the immediate slope with a stick, committing each step downhill without seeing the deeper valley visible to us through a break in the fog.
tradition_approach: Observational framing, available natural light, no staging or artificial elements — the camera records a scene as it exists
